includedSources: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes (5)
skippedSources: WealthVest / Drew Dokken & Tim Pierotti (wealthvest@substack.com) - podcast-episode announcement only; body lists topics (Iran conflict resumption, new tariffs, World Cup effect, Fed balance sheet) with zero sentences of actual analysis, data, or argument — just a link out to the podcast audio

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Goldman splits the AI trade into two risks: is the pie shrinking, or just the slices?
items.idea: Goldman Sachs strategists Dominic Wilson and Vickie Chang argue most AI selloffs aren't evidence the aggregate AI thesis is cracking — they're fights over who captures the value (the 'slices'), not whether the total value exists (the 'pie'). Distributional shocks can hammer individual stocks and sectors while leaving the broad index barely dented, and low implied correlation across AI names can conceal real damage beneath a calm-looking surface. Their tell for when it stops being a slice-fight and becomes a pie-shrinking (systemic) event: semiconductors, hyperscalers, credit spreads and Treasury yields all start moving together.
items.angle: This is the framework to hold up against every single-name AI move this week — Intel popping 10% same day Alphabet craters on capex, Google's bonds vs. SpaceX's bonds. It explains why the index looks fine while individual balance sheets are getting repriced hard, and gives a concrete checklist (semis + hyperscaler credit + 10yr) for when to actually get worried.
items.tweetA: Goldman just gave the AI unwind a taxonomy: pie-shrinking risk (the trade is fake) vs slice-fighting risk (someone else eats your margin). INTC popping 10% the same day GOOGL craters on capex is slice-fighting, not pie-shrinking. Mixing the two is how you get whipsawed this earnings season.
items.tweetB: Low correlation across AI names right now isn't calm — it's Goldman's own language, damage hiding beneath the surface. The real macro tell isn't the S&P. It's whether semis, hyperscaler credit spreads, and the 10yr start moving together. Watch those three, ignore the index level.

charts.desc: Paywalled after the opening section — Goldman's full cross-asset note almost certainly carries semis/hyperscaler/credit/yield dashboards. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: A Markov-switching model puts 93% odds the US has entered a 'high productivity' regime
items.idea: Stripe Economics' Ernie Tedeschi flags a Markov-switching model — a tool for judging when an economic series has meaningfully changed behavior — showing a 93% probability the US economy has shifted into a structurally 'high' productivity period, not just a cyclical bounce.
items.angle: This is the only number that credibly underwrites the AI capex wall of spending everyone's now questioning (Google's $205B guide, Big Tech's $1.65T debt pile). If the regime shift is real, capex has a macro justification. If it's noise, the credit market repricing hyperscaler spreads this week is the more honest signal.
items.tweetA: A Markov-switching model just put 93% odds the US economy has regime-shifted into 'high productivity.' If that's real, it's the only number that justifies a $205B Google capex guide without calling it a bubble. If it's noise, the credit market repricing hyperscaler spreads this week is closer to the truth.
items.tweetB: Nobody's connecting these two data points: a 93% probability of a US productivity regime shift landing the same week hyperscaler credit spreads are blowing out on capex worries. Either the productivity story funds the AI spend, or the bond market is right and it doesn't.

charts.desc: Markov-switching model chart plotting the probability trajectory up to the current 93% 'high productivity' regime reading.

items.title: New business formations with high job-creation propensity are ticking up
items.idea: Daily Chartbook flags a 'notable improvement' in new US business applications specifically weighted toward those with a high propensity to create jobs — a forward-looking formation signal, not a lagging employment print.
items.angle: Business formation data leads hiring by roughly two to three quarters. This lands the same week markets are pricing a 35%+ chance of a July Fed hike on inflation fears — the formation data and the hike case are telling two different stories about where the labor market is headed.
items.tweetA: New business formations with high job-creation propensity just picked up — while markets are pricing a 35%+ chance of a July Fed hike. Formation data leads employment by 2-3 quarters. The hike case and the startup data are telling two different stories about 2027.
items.tweetB: Formation data doesn't get tweeted because it's boring until it's not. High-propensity business applications ticking up now means hiring in Q4/Q1. File this against every 'labor market is cracking' take you're about to see through this earnings season.

charts.desc: Business application formation chart via @vrntperception showing the recent uptick in high-propensity-to-hire filings.

items.title: Real wages are getting squeezed — posted wage growth flat at 2.4% while the price gap 'widened considerably'
items.idea: Indeed's Wage Growth Tracker shows posted wages up 2.4% YoY through June, matching the prior three-month rate. But advertised wages have been growing slower than prices since mid-2025, and the recent inflationary run-up (oil-driven) has widened that gap 'considerably,' per Indeed's own characterization.
items.angle: This is a worse setup for consumer-discretionary names than the 'resilient labor market' headline suggests — wage growth stalling while CPI reaccelerates on oil is real-wage compression, not a cooling labor market. It also strengthens the case that a July hike would be fighting a supply shock with a demand tool.
items.tweetA: Indeed's wage tracker is flat at 2.4% YoY while the CPI gap 'widened considerably' on the recent oil-driven inflation run-up. Real wages are getting squeezed by oil, not by a cooling labor market. That's a worse setup for consumer discretionary than a soft-landing wage story.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching jobless claims (187k, a new multi-year low). Nobody's watching that the wage-price gap just widened 'considerably' per Indeed. Tight labor market + falling real wages, at the same time — that's the Fed's July-hike case and the recession case, both true at once.

charts.desc: Indeed Wage Growth Tracker line chart — posted vs. advertised wage growth against the widening CPI gap since mid-2025.

items.title: Asset managers' long VIX positioning sits in the 1st percentile — right as two oil chokepoints go live
items.idea: Nomura data (via @neilsethinew) shows asset manager long positioning in VIX futures at the 1st percentile of its historical range — essentially no institutional volatility hedge on — at the same moment Hormuz and the Red Sea are both under simultaneous threat and the Nasdaq just dropped roughly 2.5% in a session.
items.angle: This isn't a directional bearish signal, it's a convexity signal: institutions are almost entirely unhedged into a live two-chokepoint oil escalation. The next headline out of Hormuz or the Red Sea doesn't get absorbed calmly by a book positioned like this — it gets air-pocketed.
items.tweetA: Asset managers' long VIX positioning is in the 1st percentile — right as Hormuz and the Red Sea both have live chokepoint threats and the Nasdaq just dropped 2.5% in a session. Nobody's hedged. That's not a bearish signal, that's a convexity signal.
items.tweetB: 1st percentile long-VIX positioning among asset managers means the next Hormuz or Red Sea headline doesn't get absorbed calmly — it gets air-pocketed through an unhedged book. Cheap tail hedges here aren't a trade, they're insurance nobody's buying.

charts.desc: Nomura positioning chart via @neilsethinew showing asset-manager long VIX futures positioning at its 1st percentile.

items.title: Hyperscaler bond spreads are widening — the AI capex debate just moved from equity to credit
items.idea: UniCredit (via @wallstjesus) flags that hyperscaler credit spreads have widened recently, 'reigniting investor debate about the viability of their business models' given how abundant their capex has become.
items.angle: This is the same signal showing up from three independent sources in one news cycle — UniCredit on hyperscaler spreads, Nikkei on Big Tech's $1.65T total debt pile, and SpaceX's own 2056 bonds dropping 10 points same-day. Credit is starting to price AI capex risk before equity is.
items.tweetA: Three different sources today — UniCredit on hyperscaler spreads, Nikkei on Big Tech's $1.65T debt pile, and SpaceX's 2056 bonds down 10 points — are all describing the same trade: AI capex is becoming a credit story, not just an equity one. Watch spreads, not just stock prices.
items.tweetB: Hyperscaler credit spreads widening 'reignites debate about business model viability' — translation: bond investors are pricing execution risk equity investors are still ignoring. Credit usually front-runs equity by a quarter. Worth taking the hint.

charts.desc: UniCredit chart via @wallstjesus on hyperscaler bond spread widening.

items.title: Secret Service pulls a member of Vance's protective detail over a suspected leak — amid the highest threat environment on record
items.idea: The Secret Service removed a member of VP Vance's protective detail and opened an administrative investigation into a suspected leak of travel/operational security data, with a possible criminal referral. Director Sean Curran says the agency has opened more than 10,000 threat investigations this year — up roughly 40% — calling it the highest threat environment of his career.
items.angle: Not a direct market story, but a hard data point on domestic threat-level escalation rising in lockstep with the external one (Hormuz, Ukraine, Russia). Worth tracking as a proxy for how stretched the broader US security apparatus is right as multiple fronts go hot simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Secret Service threat investigations are up 40% this year — 10,000+ opened, the highest in the director's career — and now there's a suspected insider leak inside the VP's own detail. Domestic security stress is rising in lockstep with the external one (Hormuz, Ukraine). That's one threat environment, not two.
items.tweetB: A leaked travel package on a sitting VP's protective detail, during the highest threat-volume year on record. Forget the politics — the operational read is that information compartmentalization inside the US security apparatus is straining at the exact moment external threats are peaking too.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: SpaceX's equity AND its 30-year bonds are repricing together — that's a discount-rate story, not a sentiment story
items.idea: SpaceX shares fell to a fresh low near $111, down 44% from the June peak (market cap ~$1.48T, erasing ~$1.16T, now below the $135 IPO price). Its 6.65% senior unsecured bonds due 2056 — part of a $25B debut offering that drew nearly $89B of orders — have dropped from ~97 to ~87.6 cents on the dollar. Ortex estimates shorts are sitting on ~$15.5B in paper profits since IPO, with ~360M shares (56% of the free float) on loan.
items.angle: Equity-only selloffs can be waved off as sentiment. When 30-year debt reprices at the same time, the market is repricing the discount rate on distant cash flows — a structurally different (and harder to reverse) verdict than 'the stock got ahead of itself.'
items.tweetA: SpaceX stock -44% from peak is a sentiment story. SpaceX's 2056 bonds falling from 97 to 87.6 cents is a discount-rate story. When equity AND 30-year debt reprice together, the market isn't saying 'overhyped' — it's saying the cash flows aren't worth what was paid at these rates.
items.tweetB: 56% of SpaceX's free float is on loan to shorts sitting on ~$15.5B in paper profits since IPO. That's not a crowded short — that's a crowded consensus. Which is exactly why a violent Starship-launch relief rally is still on the table for anyone with that position size.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (likely an equity-vs-bond-price divergence chart in the full Substack post).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Hormuz just became a two-chokepoint story — Houthis strike Saudi tankers in the Red Sea
items.idea: Trump has warned any Iranian strike on Hormuz shipping will be answered by destroying an Iranian bridge or power plant; Iran's Ghalibaf counters that regional infrastructure won't stay secure if Iran's isn't. A tanker was left burning in Hormuz, Kuwait was hit by drones, and the Houthis claimed strikes on two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea — opening a second front. The UK has evacuated its remaining diplomatic staff from Iran, the same signal that preceded February's war outbreak; Bulgaria's reported support for US operations risks pulling in a NATO/EU member directly.
items.angle: The market's 'reroute around Hormuz' bull case for capped oil prices needs BOTH the Gulf and the Red Sea route to stay open. Today one of them started closing too — that changes the reroute math the whole 'contained' narrative has been leaning on.
items.tweetA: The oil market has been trading a one-chokepoint story (Hormuz). Today it became two: Houthis are now hitting Saudi tankers in the Red Sea while Hormuz still burns. The 'ships can just reroute' bull case for capped oil prices needs both doors open. One's already closing.
items.tweetB: The UK just evacuated its remaining diplomatic staff from Iran. The same move happened right before February's war broke out. Markets are pricing this week as noise. The people with embassy-level intelligence access are not treating it as noise.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Intel's beat is being read as 'AI capex is real' — but the actual turnaround bet (foundry) still has zero disclosed customers
items.idea: Intel posted adjusted EPS of 42 cents vs. 21 cents expected, revenue of $16.1B vs. $14.42B consensus — its fastest sales growth since 2011. Guidance for the current quarter (38 cents EPS, $15.8-16.8B revenue) topped Street estimates of 27 cents and $15.1B. Shares jumped ~10% after hours. Capex is guided higher next year to build out the foundry business, but Intel still hasn't disclosed a major external foundry customer.
items.angle: The rally is being driven by the CPU/legacy business beating easy numbers, not by the foundry turnaround thesis the stock is actually supposed to be worth a premium for. That's the wrong line item getting credit.
items.tweetA: Intel's beat (42c vs 21c expected, fastest sales growth since 2011) is getting read as 'AI capex is real.' Fine. But the stock popped 10% on a CPU story while the actual turnaround bet — foundry — still has ZERO disclosed external customers. Wrong line item is driving the rally. $INTC
items.tweetB: Alphabet goes FCF-negative on capex, market panics. Intel beats because someone has to build the chips for that capex, market cheers. Same AI spending cycle, opposite stock reaction, same day — that's Goldman's 'slices, not pie' framework playing out in real time. $INTC $GOOGL

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Goldman's own numbers say a July Fed hike barely touches the actual source of inflation
items.idea: July hike odds are priced above 35%. Goldman's David Mericle argues tariffs, war, and supply effects account for most of the current inflation overshoot — meaning a hike would need a large rise in unemployment to meaningfully offset it, since it does little to address the supply-side sources directly. The immediate transmission would hit the dollar, the Treasury curve, duration, AI funding costs and credit long before the underlying supply problem (oil, tariffs) is resolved.
items.angle: This is the strongest case yet that a July hike would be a credibility move dressed as inflation-fighting — hiking into a supply shock just adds a second, self-inflicted demand shock on top of it, hitting AI funding costs directly.
items.tweetA: Goldman's own numbers say tariffs, war and supply effects — not demand — explain most of this inflation overshoot. A July hike doesn't touch any of those. It just crushes demand until unemployment rises enough to fake an offset. That's not fighting inflation, that's manufacturing a recession to look tough.
items.tweetB: 35%+ priced odds of a July hike, and the transmission runs straight through the dollar, the long end, AI funding costs and credit — before it ever touches the actual inflation source (oil, tariffs). The Fed can move markets faster than it can move Hormuz. That mismatch is the trade.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Goldman/Bloomberg dashboard likely embedded in the paid section).

items.title: Google's FCF went negative for the first time in decades — but its cloud backlog hit $514B
items.idea: Google raised its 2026 AI capex guide to as much as $205B (from $190B+) and went free-cash-flow negative for the first time in a long time (FT: ~$6B cash burn this quarter), backstopped by an $80B+ equity raise — even while still generating $112B+ in profit. Bloomberg reports the cloud services backlog ballooned to $514B. Parekh's framing: cheaper Gemini Flash models don't shrink AI demand via commoditization — via the Jevons Paradox, cheaper compute expands the market instead.
items.angle: The bear signal (FCF negative) and the bull signal ($514B backlog) are both true at once — the stock reaction is really a referendum on which one the market weights more. Jevons Paradox is the argument for why the backlog number should matter more over time as models get cheaper, not less.
items.tweetA: Google's FCF went negative for the first time in decades and the algo sold it. But the cloud backlog hit $514B. A $6B cash burn against $514B in contracted future revenue isn't a warning sign — it's a working-capital timing problem for a company sitting on $112B of profit. $GOOGL
items.tweetB: The Jevons Paradox point nobody's pricing: Google shipped cheaper Gemini Flash models the same week it raised capex to $205B. Cheaper AI doesn't shrink the spend — it's what grows the backlog. Every 'AI is getting commoditized, capex will fall' take has this exactly backwards.

items.title: Big Tech's AI debt hit $1.65T, up 8x in four years — and Meta's off-balance-sheet piece alone is $420B
items.idea: A Nikkei Asia study of Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta and Oracle puts combined on- and off-balance-sheet AI debt/equity at roughly $1.65T, up ~8x in four years. On-balance-sheet debt is about $1.35T; the rest sits in SPVs — Meta's off-balance-sheet debt alone is pegged at ~$420B, about 3x its recorded balance-sheet debt, funneled through PE/VC-run special-purpose vehicles for institutional data-center investment.
items.angle: The real 2008 parallel here isn't the SPV structure itself (those are normal institutional vehicles, not CDO shenanigans) — it's whether index committees fast-tracking fresh AI IPOs into major indices forces passive flows in without investors getting a real choice. That's the mechanic worth watching, not the debt total.
items.tweetA: Big Tech's AI debt is up 8x in four years to $1.65T, and $420B of Meta's alone sits off-balance-sheet in SPVs. Everyone's calling that the 2008 parallel. It isn't — SPVs are normal. The real 2008 parallel is index committees fast-tracking fresh AI IPOs into major indices in weeks, forcing passive inflows into an unstress-tested story.
items.tweetB: $1.65T in Big Tech AI debt sounds scary until you split it: $1.35T on-balance-sheet against $112B+ in Google profit alone, vs. SPV structures institutional money chose to enter. The bubble tell isn't the leverage total — it's whether passive index flows get forced in without a choice. Watch index-inclusion mechanics, not the debt number.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Nikkei Asia study likely includes a debt build-up chart).

items.title: Ford is putting Apple Maps inside a new EV's operating system — not CarPlay, baked in
items.idea: Ford is building Apple Maps into a new ~$30,000 'skunk works' EV at the OS level for next year — a first, distinct from the usual smartphone-mirrored CarPlay/Android Auto. GM has dropped CarPlay entirely; Tesla runs its own vertical stack. Apple has spent years as the #2 in maps behind Google, while Chinese automakers (Xiaomi, Geely) race on the same in-car OS layer.
items.angle: This isn't really a maps story — it's a distribution bet on who owns the default AI-assistant surface inside the car. A sub-$60B automaker choosing to build on a ~$5T Big Tech partner's stack instead of its own signals the in-car AI battle just got a real non-Tesla, non-GM entrant.
items.tweetA: Ford just put Apple Maps into a $30K EV at the OS level — not CarPlay, baked in. That's not a maps story, that's Ford picking a side in the in-car AI assistant war before it's even been fought. GM went the opposite way and killed CarPlay entirely. Two automakers, two opposite bets on who owns your voice queries at 70mph.
items.tweetB: A sub-$60B Ford choosing to build on a ~$5T Apple's mapping stack instead of its own is the clearest signal yet that automakers have given up trying to out-software Big Tech. Expect this pattern to repeat with AI copilots — legacy auto becomes the hardware layer, Big Tech keeps the software margin.

items.title: ARKG returned 73% trailing 12 months — 3.4x the S&P Healthcare Index
items.idea: The ARK Genomic Revolution ETF (ARKG) returned 73.03% (NAV) over the trailing twelve months through 6/30/2026, versus 19.90% for the S&P 500 Healthcare Index and 21.34% for the MSCI World Index — more than 3x the healthcare benchmark. ARK frames the driver as falling sequencing costs converging with AI to make biology 'readable, writable, and increasingly predictable,' with exposure via names like Twist Bioscience, Tempus AI, CRISPR Therapeutics and Intellia.
items.angle: A 3.4x outperformance gap over generic healthcare beta is a decoupling signal, not stock-picking noise — AI-native genomics is starting to trade as its own factor, and anyone still benchmarking biotech picks against XLV is using the wrong yardstick.
items.tweetA: ARKG returned 73% trailing twelve months vs. 20% for S&P Healthcare. That's not stock-picking alpha, that's a regime change — AI-native genomics (Tempus, CRISPR Tx, Intellia) has decoupled from generic healthcare beta. Anyone still benchmarking biotech picks against $XLV is using the wrong yardstick.
items.tweetB: Falling sequencing costs + AI reading/writing biology is the same S-curve pattern that hit software compute costs pre-LLM boom. ARKG's 3.4x outperformance over global healthcare says the market is starting to price that curve into genomics names. Early, not over.

items.title: Intel and AMD just flipped from price-takers to price-setters with Chinese server customers
items.idea: As hardware prices soar, Intel and AMD — usually rivals — now share negotiating leverage over Chinese server customers for the first time in years; buyers are no longer in a position to dictate terms.
items.angle: This is a pricing-power reversal buried in one line, distinct from the AI capex sustainability debate everyone's fixated on — it's a direct margin tailwind for both chipmakers regardless of how the capex argument resolves.
items.tweetA: Buried single line in TheStreet: Intel and AMD 'just got leverage they haven't had in years' over Chinese server customers because hardware prices are so tight buyers can't dictate terms anymore. That's a pricing-power story completely separate from the AI capex debate — and it hits margins directly. $INTC $AMD
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether hyperscaler capex is sustainable. Nobody's pricing that the chip suppliers on the other side of that spend just flipped from price-takers to price-setters with Chinese buyers. Supply tightness doesn't care about your capex-cycle thesis.

items.title: Newmont could retire a huge chunk of its float in 2026 — and TD Cowen's production estimate already beats management's own guidance
items.idea: Newmont is down ~5.5% YTD but outperforming the HUI Gold Bugs Index (-13% YTD) on balance-sheet strength and buybacks. TD Cowen upgraded Newmont to buy on July 14 ($127 PT): trading at 0.87x NAV vs. a 3-year average of 1.10x, ~$3.7B net cash, capacity to repurchase $5B+ of stock in 2026 against a $6B authorization, and a 5.39M oz 2026 production estimate — above Newmont's own 5.26M oz guidance. Newmont reports Q2 earnings after the close today, kicking off gold-producer earnings season.
items.angle: Newmont has stopped trading like a pure gold-beta name and started trading like a capital-return re-rating story — the buyback math (potentially retiring a large chunk of float in a year) matters more for the stock right now than the gold price itself.
items.tweetA: Newmont could buy back over $5B of stock in 2026 off $3.7B net cash and a $6B authorization — against a market cap that's fallen 5.5% YTD while trading at 0.87x NAV vs. a 1.10x 3-year average. This has stopped being a gold-price trade and started being a capital-return re-rating trade. $NEM
items.tweetB: TD Cowen's production estimate for Newmont (5.39M oz) is ABOVE the company's own guidance (5.26M oz) — analysts modeling upside to management's own number, into earnings tonight, in a sector down 13% YTD. That's the setup for a beat-and-squeeze, not a value trap.

charts.desc: NEM vs. HUI Gold Bugs Index chart (Dec 2025-Jul 2026) showing relative outperformance, plus a separate Newmont daily price chart with buyback-support annotation.

items.title: HFIR's math: Iran can shut in 6M b/d of everyone else's oil while keeping its own 2M b/d flowing untouched
items.idea: HFIR's escalation math: if Iran contests the Oman route through Hormuz, roughly 6M b/d of flow gets cut off. UAE exports, which surged to ~4M b/d in June on reroute optimism, would fall back to ~2M b/d. Iran, meanwhile, keeps exporting its own ~2M b/d as if nothing happened. The author's standing thesis since a July 4 memo ('It's Now or Never') is that the US can never cede Hormuz control to Iran — making further escalation, not de-escalation, the more likely path.
items.angle: This pairs directly with tonight's Red Sea escalation (Boom Daily, item 9): the asymmetry HFIR describes — Iran can inflict 6M b/d of pain on everyone else while its own 2M b/d keeps flowing — is exactly the kind of leverage that makes the market's 'peace deal' pricing look premature.
items.tweetA: HFIR's math: if Iran contests the Oman route, ~6M b/d of OTHER people's oil gets cut off while Iran keeps exporting its own ~2M b/d untouched. That's not mutually assured destruction, that's asymmetric leverage. Every 'de-escalation' headline needs to explain why Iran gives that up voluntarily. It hasn't.
items.tweetB: UAE exports surged to 4M b/d in June on rerouting optimism. HFIR says a real Hormuz contest sends that back to 2M b/d overnight. The market's been trading the reroute as a permanent fix. It was always a temporary patch that depended on Iran not contesting the exact route it's now contesting.
